name: Hippolyta
description: Hippolyta, in Greek mythology, was a queen of the Amazons, a tribe of warrior women renowned for their strength, courage, and independence. She is most famously known for her girdle, a symbol of her authority and power, which was bestowed upon her by her father, Ares, the god of war. Hippolyta's reign over the Amazons was marked by both military prowess and a degree of diplomacy, as evidenced by her interactions with figures like Heracles. While often portrayed as a formidable warrior, accounts of Hippolyta's character vary across different sources, sometimes depicting her as a noble ruler and other times as a more aggressive and vengeful figure. Her story highlights the complex and often contradictory nature of female figures in Greek mythology, reflecting societal anxieties and aspirations regarding gender roles and power dynamics. The myths surrounding Hippolyta offer valuable insights into the cultural significance of the Amazons and their enduring legacy in Western literature and art.
subtitle: Amazonian Queen

keyMyths.title: The Ninth Labor of Heracles: Hippolyta's Girdle
keyMyths.description: As his ninth labor, Heracles was tasked with retrieving Hippolyta's girdle for Admete, daughter of King Eurystheus. Various accounts exist, some stating Hippolyta willingly offered the girdle to Heracles, charmed by his strength and valor. However, Hera, ever the antagonist, spread rumors among the Amazons that Heracles intended to abduct their queen, leading to a fierce battle. Heracles, believing he had been betrayed, slew Hippolyta and took the girdle by force, fulfilling his labor but at a great cost.

keyMyths.title: Theseus and the Amazonian War
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the myth, Theseus, the king of Athens, accompanied Heracles on his quest for the girdle or launched his own expedition to the land of the Amazons. Theseus either abducted Hippolyta during the conflict or was gifted her as a spoil of war. This act ignited a war between Athens and the Amazons, with the Amazons invading Attica to rescue their queen. The conflict ended with a truce, but the abduction of Hippolyta remained a contentious point in Athenian-Amazonian relations.

keyMyths.title: Hippolyta and Antiope
keyMyths.description: The identity of the Amazonian queen taken by Theseus varies across accounts. Some narratives identify her as Antiope, Hippolyta's sister, who willingly went with Theseus and bore him a son, Hippolytus. This version presents a more nuanced perspective, suggesting a possibility of consent and even affection between the Athenian king and the Amazonian warrior. However, this also led to further conflict when the Amazons attempted to rescue Antiope, highlighting the tensions between their culture and the patriarchal Greek society.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing accounts of various myths, including Heracles' labors and the story of Hippolyta.
sources.text: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing accounts of various myths, including Heracles' labors and the story of Hippolyta.

sources.author: Plutarch
sources.description: A biographical account of Theseus, detailing his involvement with the Amazons and the controversy surrounding the abduction of Hippolyta or Antiope.
sources.text: A biographical account of Theseus, detailing his involvement with the Amazons and the controversy surrounding the abduction of Hippolyta or Antiope.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: A universal history that includes accounts of the Amazons and their interactions with Greek heroes, offering different perspectives on the Hippolyta myth.
